course_description: This course aims to set students on the path to becoming effective decision makers. It equips students with the core economic principles that are necessary to understand how decision makers make choices and how choices can be made that make the best possible use of limited available resources. Whether you are operating in the role of a consumer apportioning your income, a company manager striving to maximise profits, or a government policy-maker seeking to tackle environmental degradation, these principles are fundamental to effective decision making.

requirements.restrictions: This course is not available to students enrolled in Economics programs.
requirements.assumed_background: There is a mathematical requirement for entry into degree programs offered by the Faculty of Business, Economics and Law. The mathematical requirements for this course are modest. However, just as in most knowledge-intensive private and public sector jobs, basic arithmetic and an ability to understand and manipulate simple graphs and equations are required in order to make good progress. No previous study in the field of economics, micro or macro, is assumed or required.

timetable:

Timetable Variation, 3 May : Please note that Monday 2 May timetable will be taught on Tuesday 3 May. Classes originally scheduled for Tue 3 May will not proceed.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: ECON1011 aims to provide an introduction to economics. It outlines the theory of markets with relevant applications to business, social and individual issues. The course covers the main principles of micro and macroeconomics.

learning_outcomes.description: Explain and communicate fundamental micro and macroeconomic theories.
Analyse and interpret economic information presented in graphs.
Calculate mathematical equations in solving economic problems.
Evaluate economic policies and the logic behind these policies.
Apply economic concepts and principles in real life situations.

assessment_details.title: Playconomics Videogame/Academia Questions
assessment_details.weight: 30%; max. 25% from either the videogame or the Academia questions
assessment_details.task_description:
In addition to the textbook (made available in both pdf and ebook versions), the Playconomics package comes with an interactive videogame (‘Game’) and also an extensive database of practice questions ('Academia').

Fully completing both these two Playconomics items is worth a maximum of 30% of the final grade. Each Playconomics item individually can contribute a maximum of 25% towards the final grade. For example, a student can earn 25 Academia marks and 5 Game marks, or 5 Academia marks and 20 Game marks, or 15 marks of each - in all cases, the student will earn the full 30% from this Playconomics assessment. (Note that a student cannot earn MORE than 30% in total from this assessment.)

Academia:

A student can earn up to 25% towards the final grade from Academia.

Academia is an extensive database of Multiple Choice and Short Answer practice questions.

A correct answer to an Academia practice question is worth a certain number of points, while an incorrect answer might 'cost' (i.e., detract) points.

The conversion rate of Academia points to course marks is will be announced at the start of the semester.

The final Academia points count will be recorded as of the date and time set up as deadline for Academia.

The Game:

A student can earn up to 25% towards the final grade from the Game.

The Game is a computer-based immersive interactive experience that plays like a videogame and teaches like your standard economics textbook. It combines gamification, personalized feedback and experiential learning in an innovative, yet accurate, way to make your Economics learning more engaging and fun. As the only educational videogame in economics, you are the first to play it!

The Game covers all the course material taught during the semester . This is a serious videogame, so the course content is delivered by placing you into an imaginary world populated by various economic agents that ‘live’ in various parts of the Playconomics world map, which features a certain number of places, goods and agents. Your mission is to achieve certain economic objectives for your economy. To do so, you will have to make economically sound decisions at the individual level (what to produce and how to face the market, how much to consume, whether to trade or not), as well as become a mayor and impose taxes, give subsidies, invest in public goods (schools, hospitals, defence) or handle externalities.

The conversion rate of Game objectives to course marks will be announced progressively during the semester.

The final Game objectives count will be recorded as of the date and time set up as deadline for the Game.

Both Academia and the Game take time to complete; do not leave it too late to start!!!

Throughout the semester, you will be provided with constant feedback on your decisions. You will also be able to access the textbook chapters and the educational videos (via the ebook) and quickly interact with the teaching staff and your peers (through the comments page).

The beginning and ending due dates of Academia and the Game (TBC, all times are in Brisbane time):

Academia Questions

Open from Monday 21st February, 2022 at 12:00noon until Tuesday 31st May, 2022 at 4:00pm

Playconomics Game

assessment_details.title: Policy Pitch
assessment_details.task_description:
Students create teams early in the semester. Each team will be allocated a policy problem that they have to analyse using economic principles covered in this course. Each team will write a policy brief outlining and justifying their proposed policy response based on economic principles and supported by very limited independent research. Each team will then make an oral presentation to pitch their policy proposal, followed by a Q&A session.

Format

The written policy brief should be a typed document of 2 pages maximum (font size 12, single-line spacing, 2cm margins on each side, excluding bibliography).

The oral policy pitch will be 6 minutes for a team of 3 students + 5 minutes question time. (Smaller/larger teams time is adjusted proportionally, i.e., 2 minutes per student.)

Teams will present via Zoom. Simply reading (out of a script) is not allowed. All team members should participate equally in the presentation.

All presentations will be recorded. If you do not wish your image and/or voice to be captured by the recording, please contact the course coordinator at the time of timeslot allcoation to make alternative arrangements.

Submission and due dates

The written policy brief should be submitted via Blackboard by Friday 13th May, 2022 4:00pm.

Teams will present in allocated timeslots.

This textbook has been especially created for this course. The textbook examinable content is defined in the Course Schedule, and includes all Revision Questions. The required textbook is available as part of the Playconomics package.

The Playconomics package is accessible via the corresponding Playconomics page on the course website. On this page, students can register and create their own account.

From their account students will be able to purchase the complete version of Playconomics. This package contains the required e-book (with integrated educational videos, Revision Questions, etc.) and the game (all levels/islands) students need to complete. Students who are unable or unwilling to purchase a license will be able to access the game using selected PCs in our on-campus computer lab. Please contact the Course Administrator for further information.

For any technical issues, please email support@lionsheartstudios.com

learning_activities.topic: Fri Seminar 1 - Introduction : What is economics, microeconomics, macroeconomics? Introduction to key microeconomics concepts. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Pindyck; Playconomics
Fri PAUSE WEEK
Mon - Fri PAUSE WEEK
Fri Seminar 2 - Comparative Advantage : Comparative advantage, specialisation and trade. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Pindyck; Playconomics
Mon - Fri Workshop 1 : Introduction to Playconomics Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Playconomics

Fri Seminar 3 - Supply : Marginal Product. Marginal Cost. Short run vs long run. Returns to scale. Principles of profit maximisation. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Pindyck; Playconomics
Mon - Fri Workshop 2 : Comparative Advantage Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Fri Seminar 4 - Demand : Decreasing marginal value. Consumer demand. The "law of demand". Factors affecting demand and elasticities. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Pindyck; Playconomics
Mon - Fri Workshop 3 : Supply Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Fri Seminar 5 Equilibrium : Introduction to market equilibrium analysis how much gets traded and at what price? Consumer and producer surpluses. Competitive market static analysis. Long run supply analysis. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Pindyck; Playconomics
Mon - Fri Workshop 4 : Demand Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Fri Seminar 6 - Govt Intervention & Internat. Trade : Efficiency, equity and the political spectrum. Market intervention, price floors, price ceilings, taxes, subsidies and deadweight loss inefficiencies. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Pindyck; Playconomics
Mon - Fri Workshop 5 : Equilibrium Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Fri Seminar 7 Monopoly : Types of market power and imperfect competition. Sources of monopoly power. Monopoly profit maximisation. Types of price discrimination. Note: Friday 15 April is a public holiday, so a pre-recorded lecture will be made available on the course Blackboard site.

Mon - Fri Workshop 6 : Government Intervention & International Trade. Note: Friday 15 April is a Public holiday, therefore no classes will be held on this day. Students are invited to view "online" workshop or attend an alternative online workshop for this week only. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
Mon - Sun Mid-Semester Break : There will be no classes this week.
Mon - Sun Mid-Semester Break : There will be no classes this week.

Fri Seminar 8 Oligopoly & Intro to Game Theory : Oligopolistic strategy. Implicit and explicit collusion. Introduction to game theory: payoff structures, dominant and dominated strategies, Nash equilibria. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Pindyck; Playconomics

Mon - Fri Workshop 7 : Monopoly. Note: Monday 25 April is a Public holiday, therefore no classes will be held on this day. Students are invited to view "online" workshop or attend an alternative online workshop for this week only. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Fri Seminar 9 Externalities & Public Goods : Private vs social costs and benefits in supply and consumption. How externalities affect market efficiency. The Coase Theorem. Approaches to dealing with externalities. Rivalry and excludability. Pure and private public goods. Government intervention in public goods markets. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Pindyck; Playconomics

Mon - Fri Workshop 8 : Oligopoly & Introduction to Game Theory. Note: Monday 2 May is a Public holiday, therefore no classes will be held on this day. Monday 2 May timetable will be taught on 3 May. Classes originally scheduled for Tue 3 May will not proceed. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Fri Seminar 10 Macro Fundamentals & AD-AS Models : What is macroeconomics and what are macroeconomic policy goals? GDP and its components. Circular flow models. Consumer price indexing. Business cycles. Aggregate demand and aggregate supply in the short run and in the long run. AD-AS in the labour market. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
Mon - Fri Workshop 9 : Externalities and Public Goods Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Fri Seminar 11 Money & Inflation : What is money? Financial institutions, monetary policy and money market operations, commercial banks and the creation of money, fractional banking and money creation, inflation. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
Mon - Fri Workshop 10 : Macroeconomics Fundamentals & Aggregate Demand and Aggregate Supply Models Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
Fri Seminar 12 Fiscal Policy : Fiscal policy, unemployment, inflationary pressure, population growth and the business cycle. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
Mon - Fri Workshop 11 : Money & Inflation Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
Fri Seminar 13 Balance of Trade and Review : Balance of trade. Balance of payment. Exchange rate. Purchasing Power Parity. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Playconomics
Mon - Fri Workshop 12 : Fiscal Policy Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Modules - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Modules (AIM) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. From Semester 1, 2021, completion of the modules in your first semester of enrolment will be compulsory for all commencing students.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

School of Economics Assessment Guidelines

The act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another either intentionally or unintentionally. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

You must make it clear which ideas you have obtained from someone else. Superficial and minor changes do not disguise your use of the words of someone else. You commit plagiarism if you do not acknowledge the source of a direct quote, or a specific piece of writing that you have paraphrased, or even if you describe an idea or concept that you have heard or read somewhere without a reference or acknowledgement.

Examples of plagiarism are:

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence with an end reference but without quotation marks around the copied text;

Copying ideas, concepts, research results, computer codes, statistical tables, designs, images, sounds or text or any combination of these;

Paraphrasing, summarising or simply rearranging another person's words, ideas, etc without changing the basic structure and/or meaning of the text;

Referring an idea or interpretation that is not one's own without identifying whose idea or interpretation it is;

A ‘cut and paste' of statements from multiple sources;

Presenting as independent, work done in collaboration with others;

Copying or adapting another student's original work into a submitted assessment item.

Submitting the same work or recycling work in more than one course without prior permission of the course coordinator.

Importance of Referencing

Complete and accurate referencing is essential for academic writing. There are three main reasons why full referencing is essential.

The first is that when another writer’s work is used without being referenced the act of plagiarism has been committed. Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one’s own work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another and is covered by the University’s Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy . 1. The penalty for plagiarism under the Policy can range from zero marks for the assessment item to cancellation of credit for the course.

The second reason for referencing is so that anyone reading the paper can follow up any ideas or concepts that have been presented. This is especially important in academic writing where people involved in research may read the paper. One of the main ways of researching a topic is to read what other people have written and then follow up some of the references they have cited. Then, after those ‘follow-up’ papers have been read the researcher can continue the search-trail by tracking down more references cited in those follow-up papers, and so on. This is known as a bibliographical chain. This research is difficult if not impossible to complete if papers are not fully and accurately referenced.

Thirdly, referencing is important as it adds credibility to the argument that you are presenting. If an argument is to have any credibility it must be supported by evidence and that evidence must be referenced.

If a reader of your paper can read something that you have written and ask questions like; who says? Or How do you know that? Or On what do you base that comment? Then what you have written needs to be referenced. For more information on the importance of, and direction on referencing, please refer to the guides available in https://guides.library.uq.edu.au/referencing?b=g&d=a&group_id=15017 .

Consequences of Plagiarism

Students should be aware that the School of Economics does not hesitate to use the procedures available under the University of Queensland Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy to investigate suspected misconduct involving plagiarism.

The penalties imposed for cases where a finding of misconduct is made vary, based on the seriousness of the case and range across but are not limited to:

a reduction in the mark for the assessment piece or cancellation of the mark for the assessment piece;

a requirement to complete further or repeat work for the subject;

the cancellation of credit for the subject;

expulsion from the University.

Students should also be aware of the fact that when a finding of misconduct is made that this is recorded on the University’s central misconduct database.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University’s Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Policy ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-work-integrated-learning-and-work-experience ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

course_introduction:
ECON1011 is an introductory economics course, covering principles of Micro and Macroeconomics. In order to understand the workings of markets and the economy, one has to adopt an "economics mindset". This introductory course covers the fundamental principles that economists use to understand and analyse economic behaviour. Understanding these basic principles equips students for further studies in economics and business. Topics and issues covered in this course include how individuals or firms make decisions about the demand or supply of a particular product, how we can judge the relative efficiency of different types of markets, how we explain why governments may need to intervene in a particular type of market but not in others, and how some government policies are more efficient than others.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

No feedback is available at this time. We look forward to receiving your responses at the end of the semester.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
Changes to Teaching Schedule after Severe Weather Events in Southeast Queensland in late February and early March

The dates of the teaching activities above have been revised after the pause of teaching due to the severe weather events in Southeast Queensland.

Important Information - Please Read

There are no workshops on public holidays. Students with scheduled workshops on these days should attend a different workshop session during the week.

Dr Priscilla Man is the Course Coordinator and has oversight of course-related matters. Ms Kim Scott is responsible for administration matters (including workshop and seminar allocation, non-subject matter information, timetables, and examination results).

ECON1011 is a #2 unit course. The University's expectation is that a #2 unit course requires a time allocation of, on average, 10 hours per week. Seminars and workshops only account for 4 hours per week. Therefore, if you are attending all of these contact sessions, it is expected that you will be spending a further 6 hours per week in self-directed learning. This could include attending consultation sessions, reading the relevant chapter(s) of the textbook, and playing a videogame (Playconomics), as well as accessing the seminar overheads from the course Blackboard site before the seminar, etc. Please give serious consideration as to whether you are willing and able to make this time commitment before enrolling.

UQ offers seminars, workshops and consultation sessions and expects that you will be attending them. While some on-line support is available in the form of Blackboard and seminar recordings, these cannot be viewed as substitutes for preparing for, attending and actively engaging in contact sessions.

Ensure you attain the marks necessary to achieve the grade to which you aspire. Some students aspire to a 6 or 7, whilst others aspire to a 4. Our job is to clearly specify the grade cut-offs, clearly state the marking criteria for assessment and then mark all students according to these criteria. In a very large course such as ECON1011, it is inevitable that some students will end up close (e.g. within 0.5% or less) to a grade cut-off. This is not unusual or unfair. You control your own destiny - ensure you are on the right side of a grade cut-off. Students can, and do, fail ECON1011 by 0.5% or less. Marginally missing out on a grade cut-off is not grounds for a remark. Assessment results will only be revisited if a marking error has occurred (e.g. assessment has not been graded according to the stated marking criteria), or if an administrative error has occurred (e.g. results for individual assessment pieces have not been added up correctly). If you feel that either of these has occurred, contact us immediately. After each assessable item, students will be given the opportunity to view their assessment (exam-viewing session times and locations will be posted on Blackboard) and so satisfy themselves that a marking or administrative error has not occurred. The formal process of querying a course result (requesting a remark on academic grounds) is set out in the UQ Policy and Procedures Library under the Assessment policy 3.10.02 ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment ).

Make sure you check your student email and the ECON1011 Blackboard site regularly (at least every few days). All important correspondence will be communicated to students throughout the semester by student email and/or via Blackboard .

Seminars

You are expected to attend one, two-hour seminar each week. If there are circumstances that prevent you from doing so, it is your responsibility to find out and catch up on what was covered in any seminars that you miss. Seminars build on each other. This means that skipping classes can quickly impede your learning and progress in the course.

Seminar slides for each seminar will be uploaded to Blackboard prior to the seminar each week. These slides will only be a summary of what will be covered in the seminar. Downloading and printing these from Blackboard should not be seen as a substitute for seminar attendance. For example, a seminar slide may simply pose a question with the answer left to be discussed during the seminar or a corresponding workshop session.

Seminars will be recorded using UQ's lecture recording software, and subsequently made available for download on Blackboard. However, be aware that the recorded seminars only offer a visual of the Powerpoint / Visualiser presentation and an audio overlay of the lecturer's microphone. They do not capture any visual of the seminar presentation itself or any verbal interaction between the lecturer and students, which can often be extensive. Accordingly, while it can be viewed as useful supplementary material, it cannot be seen as a viable substitute for seminar attendance and should only be used for revision purposes.

Just as students have different learning styles, lecturers have different teaching styles. This may cause some frustration if you prefer one style to another. However, as lecturers we cannot hope to please everyone. Nevertheless, you are very warmly encouraged to discuss any issues you might have with this during the first part of the semester rather than waiting until the end.

Note that a seminar is not intended to be a summary of a textbook chapter. Rather, each seminar seeks to add value in some way. For example, a seminar might include the application of theory covered in the textbook to a novel, real-world situation. Seminars also help guide you on what parts of the textbook are the most relevant for assessment or other purposes. Extensions of textbook material made in lectures are assessable unless otherwise indicated.

Workshops

Weekly workshops of 50 minutes duration will commence in Teaching Week 2 . All of the workshops in any week cover the same work and are based on material covered in the previous Teaching Week's lecture. You are expected to register for and attend one workshop per week. Students are required to select their preferred class times through My Timetable and attend their allocated timeslot. (The exception is when your class falls on a public holiday). A record of workshop attendance will be kept and may be used in advising on student matters, including those involving withdrawal.

After you have been allocated to your preferred workshop, you must remain in this workshop. However, if you need to attend a different workshop in a particular week, you should seek permission from the course coordinator to do so. Attendance is monitored. Workshops are capped at 35 students to ensure an effective learning environment. For this reason, where overcrowding occurs preference will always be given to students who have signed on.

Most workshops work through a series of pre-set questions that can be found on the ECON1011 Blackboard site. You should work through the material before coming to the workshop, otherwise what you get out of the class will be limited. Do not expect your tutor to simply provide you with the answers. This is not their job. The tutor will direct questions to students during the class and active participation is encouraged.

Consultations

Consultations are your chance to speak directly with the teaching staff, one on one. A consultation timetable listing the consultation times/locations of teaching staff will be posted on the ECON1011 Blackboard site. There is no need to make an appointment for consultation and you may attend any teaching staff member's consultation session. Note that consultation sessions may be busy prior to an exam or assessment time.

Don't be shy to seek out one-on-one help from teaching staff during consultation sessions. They are there to help. Note however that staff on consultation will expect that you have been performing your side of the bargain, e.g., dedicating 10 hours per week to ECON1011, attending contact sessions, etc. If it becomes obvious that you have not, you'll likely be asked to return after you have done so.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Example criteria for each of the grades can be found in PPL 3.10.02 Assessment Procedures - section 7 Appendix.

Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Low Fail: Absence of evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

0% - 29%

Grade 2, Fail: Minimal evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

30% - 46%

Grade 3, Marginal Fail: Demonstrated evidence of developing achievement of course learning outcomes:

47% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrated evidence of functional achievement of course learning outcomes:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrated evidence of proficient achievement of course learning outcomes:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of advanced achievement of course learning outcomes:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of exceptional achievement of course learning outcomes:

85% +

Other Requirements & Comments:

A student's final overall end of semester percentage mark will be rounded to determine their final grade. For example, 64.5% rounds to 65%, while 64.4% rounds to 64%.
late_submission:
Note: Due dates listed for all assessment items have already been revised after the pause in teaching activities due to the severe weather events in Southeast Queensland. A submission will be considered late if it is made after the respective due date listed.

Requests for the granting of extensions must be made online via https://my.uq.edu.au/node/218/2#2 with supporting documentation before the submission due date/time. If an extension is approved, the new agreed date for submission will be noted on the application and the student notified through their student email. Extensions cannot exceed the number of days you suffered from a medical condition, as stated on the medical certificate.

Students who are ill for more than 14 days may wish to apply for withdrawal without academic penalty. The guidelines for late submission of progressive assessment may be accessed through http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/extension-progressive-assessment .

Where an extension has not been previously approved, the following penalties apply to late or non-submission of an assessment item (e.g. essay) in alignment with PPL 3.10.02:

Videogame and Academia Questions: Late submission is not possible. A penalty of 100% will be applied for non-submission. The videogame and questions are open for an extended period during the semester.

Team presentation:

If a team of students submit their written component of the presentation late, the following penalties will be applied to score relating to the written component across the whole team as follows: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark allocated for the assessment item will be deducted per day for up to 7 calendar days, at which point any submission will not receive any marks unless an extension has been approved. Each 24 hour block is recorded from the time the submission is due.

The presentation is a mandatory integrated component of the Policy Pitch assessment. Failing to attend or complete the oral assessment could lead to a zero mark for the Policy Pitch. Being late for an oral assessment at the allocated time will be treated as failing to attend the oral assessment.

If a student misses their scheduled presentation time due to circumstances beyond their control, they should request an extension to the Policy Pitch assessment with supporting documentation following the UQ assessment extension request process. In addition, they should also submit a written statement from their other team members confirming the absent student's contribution to the written policy brief and the preparation of the presentation. If the extension is granted, the student missing their scheduled presentation will be asked to provide a video submission to answer questions based on the presentation of the other team members. There will be no additional sessions held for students to make up their missed presentation.

Quizzes:

Access to each quiz on Blackboard will be blocked once the timer expires or as the quiz closes. A penalty of 100% will be applied for non-submission unless an extension has been granted. To apply for an extension, students must provide evidence (e.g., a medical certificate) that the late submission is beyond their control. If a student has a medical certificate covering the whole period of availability of the quiz, or any other valid reason (as defined by the University regulation) for not taking the test, he/she should inform the course coordinator as early as possible before the due date and a specific arrangement will be considered on a case-by-case basis.

If students experience interruptions to their assessments, they are required to collect suitable evidence, e.g. screenshots, photos, or emails from AskUs and advise the course coordinator.

Students can lodge a grievance to the Head of School prior to the date of finalisation of results of the course or the Associate Dean (Academic) after the date of finalisation of results of the course, in accordance with 3.60.02 Student Grievance Resolution procedures.
other_assessment_information:
Supplementary Assessment

Should you fail a course with a grade of 3, or a non-graded ‘N’, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment. Refer to my.UQ for information on supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment may not be available for all courses, or for some of the assessment items for a course. The highest grade you can receive following supplementary assessment is a 4 or P. Details of availability of supplementary assessment for this course are set out below.

Supplementary Assessment is available for this Course .

Refer to my.UQ for how to apply for supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment can take any form, for example, an oral or a written exam. Students who are eligible and approved for a supplementary assessment and the form of assessment is an examination, are expected to be available to sit the supplementary exam during the University’s Deferred and Supplementary examination period. Once approved, supplementary assessment cannot be rescinded by the student.

Other

Plagiarism

The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available in this course profile under Section 6.1 - Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines. Please refer to Section 6.1 and the link to the Academic Integrity Module (AIM) . It is strongly recommended that you complete the AIM if you have not already done so.
